content: A large language model generates text by predicting what word comes next based on patterns it has seen in vast amounts of writing. It has been trained on billions of sentences, learning which words often appear together and in what order. When you give it a prompt, the model calculates the likelihood of each possible next word and picks the one with the highest chance, repeating this step until it forms a sentence or paragraph. It doesn’t copy exact sentences but combines learned patterns in new ways, sometimes producing surprisingly coherent and relevant responses. However, the model doesn’t actually understand meaning like humans do—it’s essentially guessing based on patterns, which means mistakes and nonsensical outputs can happen. That’s why human oversight is important when using language models, especially for critical or factual content. In short, these models create text by statistically predicting word sequences from massive data, explaining both their strengths and limits.
